Introduction
Burn rehabilitation focuses on a comprehensive approach to sur -
vivorship, utilizing a multidisciplinary team of specialists to focus 
on the physical, functional, and psychological recovery of those 
surviving burn injuries. Burn rehabilitation is specialized to each 
survivor’s needs to achieve personalized outcomes and goals. Phys-
iatrists, given their unique skill set, can and should follow burn 
survivors for months to years after the initial injury.
As mortality after burn injury continues to fall (with a nearly 
98% survivorship rate),7 focus is shifting to evidence-based strate-
gies for long-term prevention of complications, optimization of 
rehabilitation, and interventions to promote psychological wellness 
and social recovery. T o achieve these goals, the National Institute on 
Disability and Rehabilitative Research has created and funded the 
Burn Model Systems database. 93 This resource, along with the 
American Burn Association’s (ABA) repository, has allowed data-
driven advances in care of individuals throughout their lifetime.
Classification
Burn injury results in tissue damage caused by thermal (flame), 
chemical, electrical, or radiation exposure. Burn injury severity 
and subsequent treatment are defined by the size and depth of the 
burn, which should be documented upon initial presentation to a 
burn center but may evolve as the burn wound develops.
Burn Size
The size of the burn is defined by the percentage of total body 
surface area (TBSA) involved. Generating a TBSA percentage is 
the basis for fluid resuscitation, transfer decisions, and prognosis 
of complications and mortality. This is typically estimated using 
the Lund-Browder chart ( Fig. 27.1 ), which approximates the 
percentage of body area in each limb, the head, torso, and genita-
lia. Utilizing different percentages based on relative size of body 
parts for young children compared to adults improves accuracy of 
TBSA calculation. Each area of burn can be further broken down 
based on the percentage of each area affected. For example, if half 
of the anterior right arm is burned, it can be estimated to involve 
0.75% TBSA. Smartphone applications, digital planimetry, and 
laser Doppler are also used in some centers.31 The Lund-Browder 
chart is most helpful for cutaneous burns from high thermal 
sources such as flame. It will not give an accurate representation 
of damage below the surface of the skin, such as that present from 
electrical injury.
needs, as well as local inflammatory and signaling pathway 
changes that affect the healing process and can lead to  
development of hypertrophic scarring.
• The physiatrist diagnoses and treats postburn complications, in-
cluding pruritis, neuropathy, heterotopic ossification, care of am-
putated limbs, pain, nutrition, dysphagia, cognitive dysfunction, 
psychological coping and mood, and community reintegration.
• The physiatrist devises and oversees an exercise program based 
on the survivor’s functional status and goals, to include aerobic 
and resistive exercises, cardiovascular conditioning, and work 
hardening.
• Psychological consequences of burn injury can include anxiety, 
depression, sleep disturbances, difficulty with body image and 
self-esteem, and changes in sexuality and sexual functioning, 
and these issues should be addressed.
• Community reintegration is a key component of burn survivors’ 
recovery.
27
Burns
MARIA F. TWICHELL
• Rehabilitation after burn injury focuses on evidence-based  
interventions to minimize complications of burn injury and  
improve outcomes.
• Physiatrists’ involvement with the interdisciplinary team treating 
burn survivors should begin at admission, continue through the 
inpatient rehabilitation and outpatient setting, and continue to 
address needs months and years after the initial injury. Physiatrists 
impact the long-term health and well-being of burn survivors.
• Acute management of burn injury is a critical component to 
survivors’ short- and long-term outcomes, and an interdisciplin-
ary team is required.
• Pain mitigation should include treatment of nociceptive and 
neuropathic pain, anxiety, and neuropathic itch via multimodal 
medications and nonpharmacologic interventions, but it  
remains challenging.
• Burn injury results in long-lasting widespread systemic effects 
to include metabolic rate alterations and unique nutritional

painful, and without exudate. They typically heal within 7 days, 
without intervention such as dressings or surgical debridement, 
as the epidermis regenerates from the basal cell layer. Superficial 
burns are not included as part of the Lund-Browder calculation 
of burn size.
Partial-thickness burns produce damage to the level of the 
dermis. The dermis is primarily composed of collagen and pro -
tein; it contains the dermal appendages such as hair follicles, 
exocrine sebaceous and sweat glands, cutaneous nerves (transmit-
ting touch, vibration, proprioception, and pain), and arterial and 
venous capillaries.
70 Superficial partial-thickness burns damage 
the upper levels of the dermis. Due to loss of the epidermis, fluid 
may be contained within a blister, or if the blister is unroofed, 
leak. Partial-thickness burns weep and are painful due to pre -
served nerves within the lower portions of the dermis. Addition-
ally, capillaries remain intact, so these burns blanch with pressure. 
Superficial partial-thickness burns may be able to reepithelize via 
basal cell migration if remnants of hair follicles and other adnexa 
that contain basal cells remain intact.
86
Deep partial-thickness burns affect the deeper layers of the 
dermis and typically do not reepithelize well due to damage to 
most of the remaining basal cell layers and persistent inflamma-
tory state.
70,86 Because of these factors, attempted healing of deep 
partial-thickness burns is prone to development of dysfunctional 
skin with hypertrophic scarring or contracture.
40 Wound care is 
initiated, but these burns typically require debridement, place-
ment of allograft or dermal substitutes, and eventual autograft for 
the best functional and cosmetic outcome. Deep partial-thickness 
burns are extremely painful.
70
Burns that breach the basement membrane of the dermis and 
expose subcutaneous fat, muscle, or bone are considered full 
thickness.
70 These burns do not blanch or produce exudate due  
to the destruction of capillaries. They may be white and waxy in 
appearance or dark from necrotic coagulated tissues. If deeper sub-
cutaneous structures such as major blood vessels are involved, limbs 
may be nonsalvageable and require amputation. Full-thickness 
burns are less painful than partial-thickness burns given destruc -
tion of cutaneous nerve fibers.
70 Standard of care is prompt exci -
sion of eschar to reduce nidus of infection, reduce inflammatory 
response attributed to necrotic tissue, and reduce potential for 
compartment syndrome.
86,141
Other injuries requiring the specialized care at a burn center 
include acute frostbite, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, toxic epider -
mal necrosis, and necrotizing fasciitis.51

Burn Depth
Burn depth is the other measure of burn severity. Burns are classi-
fied into superficial, superficial partial thickness, deep partial thick-
ness, and full thickness depending on the layers of skin involved 
(Figs. 27.2 and 27.3). Each classification has unique characteristics, 
but often multiple depths of burn can be seen in one injury.
Superficial burns involve only the epidermis. The function of 
the epidermis is to provide a barrier from external pathogens 
and prevent moisture loss.

Initial Resuscitation
In addition to defining burn size and depth, acute care is initiated 
by the receiving team per the Advanced Burn Life Support 
(ABLS) protocol.
6 ABLS includes assessment of airway (to deter -
mine presence of inhalation injury and/or need for intubation), 
breathing, and circulation, with emphasis on fluid resuscitation 
utilizing established protocols. Secondary survey for additional 
traumatic injuries, carbon dioxide levels, and pain control are also 
addressed.
Injury characteristics requiring transfer to a certified burn cen-
ter for specialized care and resuscitation are outlined in Box 27.1.
7
Electrical Burn
Electrical injury is a special consideration requiring immediate 
transfer to a certified burn center because these injuries are more 
likely to result in neuropathy, burn-related fasciotomies, amputa-
tions, and longer length of hospital stay compared to thermal  
injury.
72,159 Electrical burns are arbitrarily divided into those due 
to high voltage (.1000 V), often encountered in a workplace 
setting, and low voltage ( ,1000 V), likely to occur at home. 
Electrical injury typically accounts for approximately 5% of all 
admissions to a burn center.
160 Typically, there are relatively small 
cutaneous entry and exit points, but there may be significant 
damage to underlying structures. Central and peripheral nerves 
are particularly susceptible because nerves, by their nature, are 
electrical conductors.9 Clonus may be seen as a later-onset com -
plication of electric damage to central and peripheral nerves. 44
Muscle is also preferentially affected by electrical injury. Damage 
to the cellular lipid bilayer of myocytes leads to progressive necrosis 
and tissue edema, resulting in compression and compartment syn-
drome.160 Additional consequences of muscle and myocardial necro-
sis include rhabdomyolysis and renal failure via myoglobinuria.160 
Damage to blood vessels can result in avascular necrosis of limbs 
or end organs.
159 Electrical injuries can also cause damage to ocu-
lar structures (retina and lens); thus ophthalmology evaluation is 
required.
160
Concomitant Diagnoses
Inhalation Injury
The presence of inhalation injury (along with burn size and age) 
is one of the major predictors of mortality and should be assessed 
promptly upon presentation. In those requiring hospitalization 
for severity of the thermal burns, reported incidence of inhala -
tional injury varies from 5%
179 to 20% to 30%. 152 The risk of 
death in the presence of inhalation injury is approximately 30%, 
compared to 2% in survivors with cutaneous burns alone.
163  
In addition, there is a higher likelihood for development of com -
plications such as pneumonia, adult respiratory distress syn -
drome, and multisystem organ failure where inhalation injury is 
present.
152 Awareness of inhalation injury at the time of resuscita-
tion is helpful to predict decreased pulmonary diffusion capacity 
and respiratory muscle weakness leading to decreased exercise 
tolerance during rehabilitation.
186 Inhalation injury is also an in -
dependent risk factor for dysphagia.36
Fractures
T raumatic fractures are reported to occur in 5% to 7% of burn 
survivors presenting to burn centers and should not be overlooked 
on survey especially in those with burn injury from car crashes.
48 
Coordination of care between services is essential to reduce infec-
tion risk, ensure proper timing for fixation of fractures, and deter-
mine progression of weight-bearing status that may influence 
postacute rehabilitation.
Brain Injury
Concomitant brain injuries from direct trauma, blast effect, or 
anoxia can also complicate acute care management and rehabilita-
tion. Resultant agitation or confusion due to brain injury may 
impede survivors’ understanding and cooperation with wound 
care, range of motion (ROM), weight-bearing status, adherence 
with medical devices, and positioning.
171 The first-line interven-
tion is always standard delirium precautions. If restraints are 
needed for safety, care should be taken to avoid placing over 
burned or grafted areas. Standard medications for agitation can be 
employed, such as propranolol and quetiapine.
Surgical Management
Excision and debridement within 48 hours of burn injury has 
been found to reduce complications, intensive care unit (ICU) 
length of stay, and hospital length of stay.
141 Full-thickness, cir-
cumferential burns to the trunk or extremities are prone to tissue 
swelling resulting in compartment syndrome, requiring prompt

Criteria for Transfer to a Certified Burn 
Center
• Any burn involving the face, hands, feet, or genitals
• Inhalation injury
• Greater than 20% partial-thickness burn in those older than 10 years and 
younger than 50 years
• Greater than 10% partial-thickness burn in those younger than 10 years 
and older than 50 years
• Any full-thickness burn .5% total body surface area
• Electrical burn
• Chemical burn

fasciotomy. Excision and preparation of the wound bed may  
entail multiple trips to the operating room throughout the survi-
vor’s hospital stay depending on burn size and medical stability. 
Allograft from donated cadaveric skin or biologic synthetic dress-
ings can be applied to protect the wound bed and minimize in -
sensible losses.
74 Once the survivor is hemodynamically stable and 
the wound bed is clean and viable, autografting is pursued. Typi-
cally, split-thickness skin grafts (STSG) are harvested from an 
unaffected area of the survivors’ body. A dermatome ( Fig. 27.4) 
will remove the epidermis and a portion of the dermis. The deeper 
portions of the dermis at the donor site will provide reepitheliza -
tion of donor sites within a few weeks. The donor site is typically 
extremely painful until reepithelization occurs. The STSG is then 
meshed to increase the surface area coverage. The interstices be -
tween the meshed areas close by secondary intention. If deemed 
necessary, closure time can be augmented with epidermal auto -
grafts (ReCell, Valencia, CA) to accelerate healing.
79 In cosmeti-
cally sensitive areas, such as the hands, feet, and face, sheet grafts 
(unmeshed STSG) are used to minimize the meshed appearance 
of scarring.
87
In areas of circumferential burn to limb or torso, tissue damage 
and inflammatory swelling can lead to increased volume within a 
space surrounded by uncompressible fascial planes, leading to 
further tissue ischemia and damage referred to as compartment 
syndrome.
104 Compartment syndrome is diagnosed clinically by 
assessing pulses, palpation to evaluate for tense compartments, 
and increased pain on passive ROM.
104 Compartment syndrome 
can lead to perfusion deficit and potentially further damage to 
distal tissue and may require fasciotomy to correct.
74 If the dam-
age to the tissue from the burn injury itself or compartment 
syndrome is beyond salvage, an amputation may be considered.
Physiatric Evaluation and  
Functional Assessment
Early physical medicine and rehabilitation consult by a physiatrist 
allows involvement throughout the patient’s acute care course to 
make appropriate rehabilitation-specific recommendations and 
evaluate the patient’s candidacy for inpatient rehabilitation (IPR). 
Utilization of physical (PT) and occupational therapy (OT) ser -
vices during the acute care phase is standard of care.
When evaluating a burn survivor in the acute care setting, the 
rehabilitation team should document pertinent portions of the 
history and physical exam to inform the treatment plan. Essential 
• Fig. 27.4 Donor skin is removed from an unburned area of skin through 
the use of a powered dermatome.
elements of the history include how the burn occurred, etiology, 
classification and TBSA of the burn injury (including the initial 
modified Lund-Browder chart documented by the acute care 
team), concomitant injuries, plans for surgical salvage vs. amputa-
tion of affected limbs, weight-beating status, additional planned 
interventions or surgeries, pain and current management strategy, 
need for perioperative positioning and splinting, preinjury func-
tional status and cognition and activity level, current ability to 
perform activities of daily living (ADL) and mobility, psychoso -
cial concerns, and short- and long-term treatment goals. The 
exam should include measurement of edema, ROM, strength, 
sensation, and scar assessment.
76
T reatment paradigms are shifting away from postgraft bedrest 
toward early ambulation to minimize effects of immobility.58 Prac-
tice guidelines established in 2012 advocated for early ambulation 
(within #1 day of graft application) for those with isolated lower 
extremity skin grafts of relatively small TBSA.
113 A soft compres-
sive dressing should be in place, and grafts crossing joints should 
be splinted into neutral.
113 Ambulation on the day of lower  
extremity grafting (with a soft compressive dressing in place) did 
not result in graft loss, shear, or hematoma formation, even if grafts 
crossed a joint.
58 Several studies have also demonstrated feasibility 
of mobilizing burn survivors with larger TBSA and those requiring 
mechanical ventilation with improvement in ICU and hospital 
length of stay.
43,115 Data collected during early ambulation, such as 
level of assistance needed, endurance, pain with mobility, and joint 
ROM during ambulation, can help inform the needed rehabilita-
tion resources at the time of acute care discharge.
Pain Management After Burn
Pain after burn injury is often significantly experienced regardless 
of burn depth and size. Adequate analgesia requires a multimodal 
approach to reduce barriers to treatment and rehabilitation.
63 A 
generalized understanding of pain pathophysiology and manage-
ment is imperative to treating burn survivors.
150 An adequate pain 
management regimen should address all facets of pain, as follows:
• Background pain is present at rest and constant, due to noci -
ceptive activation as a direct result of tissue damage, pressure, 
and the inflammatory milieu.
84,92,148,182
• Episodic pain is described as an escalation of pain without an 
inciting stimulus and is of relatively short duration.
182
• Procedurally evoked pain is typically of higher intensity, al -
though reduces when the precipitating intervention (dressing 
changes, manipulation and debridement of wounds, aggressive 
stretching with therapy, operative interventions) ceases.
84 Pro-
cedural pain can be encountered multiple times per day.
• Donor site skin harvest produces new areas of exquisite pain. 148
• Anticipatory anxiety also requires treatment because it can 
heighten the pain experience.
84
Adequate treatment of acute pain is imperative to reduce cen-
tral sensitization that can progress to chronic pain and to facilitate 
participation in rehabilitation and recovery.
63,84,182 In the acute 
phase of burn treatment, opioids are the most effective means to 
mitigate moderate to severe pain.
148 Opioid selection and route  
of administration should consider individuals’ medical comor -
bidities, current level of respiratory support, and physiology 148; 
however, preference of agent tends to be center specific because  
no consensus or clinical trial data provide recommendations for 
one opiate over another in the burn population.
110 In cases of a 
burn survivor requiring intubation and sedation for physiologic 
support in the ICU or intraoperatively, continuous infusion of

opioid pain medications may be appropriate. 85 After extubation, 
intravenous administration via patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) 
pumps or intravenous push by a nurse can be used, with the goal 
to transition to an oral formulation for discharge to home or the 
next level of care. For those with hand burns, PCA controls may 
need special modifications.
While the goal is always preferential downtitration of opioids 
over other pain modulators,
148 it can be expected that those with a 
protracted course will require a high dose (and at times higher than 
recommended dose)
18 of opioid analgesia accounting for tolerance 
after prolonged exposure as well as physiologic differences in  
metabolism of opioids49 after burn injury. Well-established com-
plications of opioid treatment in the burn population are similar 
to nonburned patients, including tolerance, nausea, constipation, 
pruritis, and respiratory depression. The amount of opioids  
required by burn survivors directly correlates with longer-term 
negative outcomes such as opioid dependence or abuse, mental 
and behavioral disorders, and self-harm in this population.
180 In 
one retrospective study, 4.2% of opioid-naive burn survivors met 
criteria for long-term opiate use or opiate use disorder 1 year after  
injury,
24 further underscoring the need for adequate acute pain 
regimen with discontinuation of opioids as soon as possible.
Opioid pain medications should never be administered in 
isolation given the elevated risk of harm in this population. 147 
Instead, multimodal analgesia integrates nonpharmacologic and 
pharmacologic interventions to target diverse mechanisms of 
pain, which synergistically provide elevated pain relief compared 
with one intervention alone.
33 Burn survivors who receive multi -
modal analgesia have less opiate medication requirements while 
inpatient without increase in pain levels
47 as well as at discharge 
and at subsequent follow-up, 188 reducing potential for tolerance 
and dependence.
Adjuncts to manage acute nociceptive pain in burn survivors 
include ketamine and buprenorphine, although this varies by  
institution.84 Continuous nerve block infusions utilizing bupiva -
caine have been shown to reduce the usage of opioid pain medica-
tions after donor site harvest78 and have high-quality evidence for 
superior efficacy to opioids.33 Although not specific for pain after 
burn injury, coadministration of acetaminophen or nonsteroidal 
antiinflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) with opiates is strongly recom-
mended based on high-quality evidence.
33,148
Clinicians should also be aware of opioid-induced hyperalgesia 
(OIH), in which an increase in dosage of opiates results in higher 
levels of pain instead of amelioration. Proposed mechanisms in-
clude a complex interplay between afferent nerves, spinal cord 
dorsal horn neurons, spinal glutamate transporters, circulating 
cytokines, and modulation at the rostral ventromedial medulla.
80 
OIH may be difficult to differentiate from tolerance in burn  
survivors. Pain in OIH is described as widespread allodynia and 
hyperalgesia, even outside of the original area of pain.
80 This pain 
worsens with increased dosages of opioids. A difficult clinical 
decision to reduce the amount of opioid and assess carefully 
whether pain improves can help differentiate tolerance from OIH.
80 
Additional strategies of opioid rotation and adjunct nonopioid 
medications may be helpful. Preclinical research is ongoing to refine 
opiates to preferentially activate ligands of the mu, kappa, and 
delta nociceptive receptors that reduce transmission of pain signals 
and avoid those ligands that facilitate undesired effects.
77
Neuropathic Pain
Neuropathic components of burn-induced pain are expected given 
extensive damage to cutaneous nociceptors and nerve fibers. 
Contributors include pathologic firing of peripheral nerves as well  
as amplification of pain signals due to peripheral and central 
sensitization.
110 Neuropathic pain after burn injury is similar in de-
scription to neuropathic pain due to conditions such as diabetic and 
postherpetic neuralgia.
155 However, limited and at times conflicting 
data exist on the effects of neuromodulating medications such as ga-
bapentin and pregabalin,92,183 which are effective in nonburn neuro-
pathic pain. In both retrospective 187 and prospective studies, 85 
pregabalin in dosages up to 300 mg twice daily decreased the pain 
score in burn survivors and was well tolerated. Gabapentin dosed 
prior to surgical debridement of burn wounds demonstrates improve-
ment in pain levels and reduced opioid requirements postproce -
dure.
144 Decreased opioid requirements when gabapentin is utilized 
as part of a standard pain protocol have been reported.68,94 However, 
one small randomized and placebo-controlled study found no differ-
ence in incidence of neuropathic pain scores, postprocedural pain 
scores, opioid requirements, or functional status both during hospital 
stay and at the time of first posthospital follow-up with the addition 
of neuropathic pain medications.
183 Despite the paucity and at times 
conflicting evidence, the ABA recommendations include consider-
ation of neuropathic pain-modulating agents in those with neuro-
pathic pain features or who are refractory to standard therapy.148
Nonpharmacologic Pain Management Techniques
The ABA strongly recommends augmenting medical pain man -
agement with nonpharmacologic pain relief. 148 This can include 
cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) to reinterpret and reframe the 
pain experience, virtual reality to distract from current pain level 
by immersing into a novel environment,
14 and relaxation tech-
niques such as deep breathing or listening to music to decrease 
anticipatory anxiety that perpetuates pain.
108 Given discrepancies 
between hospital resources and methods involved in study design, 
there is no recommendation of one nonpharmacologic interven-
tion over another. However, nonpharmacologic intervention has 
not been associated with harm to burn survivors and thus is a 
strongly recommended adjunct for pain management.
148
Complications of Burn Injury
Burn injury results in a wide range of local and systemic compli-
cations. It is important for the physiatrist to recognize and treat 
these issues that will impact outcomes and function if not  
addressed. In addition, these complications may present acutely 
or manifest months to years later in recovery, further underscor-
ing the importance of long-term physiatric assessment.
Hypermetabolic State and Nutritional Support Needs
Nutrition presents a challenge within burn rehabilitation. The resting 
energy expenditure of a survivor of severe burn injury is significantly 
increased (40% to 100% more than baseline)
73 due to circulating 
catecholamines and their effect on adenosine triphosphate–driven 
metabolic processes, including protein synthesis, hepatic gluconeo-
genesis, and urea production, among others.
191 Increased mito-
chondrial oxygen consumption for thermogenesis to compensate 
for heat lost through skin disruption and thus maintain body 
temperature also plays a role.
133 Skeletal muscle catabolism out-
paces skeletal muscle synthesis, leading to decreased lean muscle 
mass, which may persist for years.
133 Optimizing nutrition includes 
early enteral feeding to offset the massive increase in metabolic rate 
that can last for 1 to 3 years.
114
Nutritional support is therefore critical to recovery and reha -
bilitation acutely and chronically after burn. T otal caloric needs

are anywhere between 20% and 60% greater than baseline. 185 
Multiple formulas have been used to determine caloric needs after 
burn injury and guide supplementation, although no consensus 
yet exists.114 Care should be taken not to overfeed when metabolic 
needs wane.
Current recommendations to decrease skeletal muscle break -
down during the hypermetabolic state of burn recovery include a 
diet that is high carbohydrate (55% of calories), moderate protein 
(20% to 25% of calories), and low fat ( ,20% of calories). 164 
High-carbohydrate components are recommended as glucose is a 
preferred energy source, sparing breakdown of intrinsic protein 
that can be utilized for wound healing and reducing muscle mass 
breakdown.114 Optimal protein intake facilitates protein synthesis 
and reduces nitrogen loss. 125 In addition, supplementation of  
vitamins and trace minerals, in particular A, E, C, D, zinc, and 
folic acid, as deficiencies are shown to lead to impaired immune 
function and wound healing with increased morbidity.164
In addition to increasing appropriately balanced caloric intake, 
several pharmacologic interventions may be used to mitigate the 
effects of the hypermetabolic state. Oxandrolone, an analog of 
testosterone, is typically dosed 10 mg twice daily and may be used 
for up to 2 years to promote protein synthesis and reduce muscle 
protein loss.42 Important benefits of oxandrolone include improv-
ing lean muscle mass, protein synthesis, bone density, and muscle 
strength and reducing time between surgeries. The 1% risk of 
hepatic dysfunction and hirsutism should be kept in mind, but 
overall, oxandrolone poses minimal risk. Despite these benefits, 
oxandrolone has now been removed from the US market but may 
be available in other countries.185 Human growth hormone shows 
similar effects on burn wound healing compared to oxandrolone, 
but the significant expense, hyperglycemic effects, and increase  
in metabolic rate have led it to fall out of favor. 22,41 Propranolol 
reduces cardiac work, liver steatosis, and muscle catabolism 
through beta-adrenergic blockade and may eventually become a 
standard of care for preserving muscle mass after burn injury. 128 
Metformin or insulin may be used for hyperglycemia and insulin 
resistance that occurs as part of the stress response to burn and 
result in poorer outcomes. The suggested goal for postburn glu -
cose levels is 130 to 140 mg/dL.2 In many cases, the combination 
of these pharmacologic interventions provides positive outcomes 
compared to controls.185
Dysphagia
Dysphagia of varying degrees was detected in 27.78% of people 
surviving a major burn. 128 Dysphagia after burns can complicate 
the provision of adequate nutrition intake and place survivors at 
risk for additional complications such as aspiration pneumonia, 
dehydration, or insufficient intake to meet their postburn nutri -
tional needs. Risk factors for dysphagia after burns include larger 
TBSA (.18%), older age, head and face involvement, presence of 
inhalation injury, need for prolonged mechanical ventilation, and 
need for tracheostomy or escharotomy. 128 Generalized muscle 
weakness and catabolism may also contribute to development. 151 
All burn survivors should have an initial screening for dysphagia, 
especially if they have had a protracted course including ventila -
tion, immobilization, or prolonged nonoral means of nutrition 
prior to diet initiation.
Objective evaluation of swallowing function can be completed 
by functional endoscopic evaluation of swallow (FEES) or modi -
fied barium swallow (MBS). For those with identified dysphagia, 
visits five to seven times per week with a trained speech-language 
pathologist (SLP) are recommended. T reatment includes standard 
oropharyngeal swallowing exercises, Frazier free water protocol, 
and trials of advanced textures in a controlled environment before 
diet upgrades.61 Some patients will need multiple evaluations to 
assess progress. Improvement in swallowing function of those 
with dysphagia is the most rapid between weeks 2 and 6 after 
injury.151 Of patients with initial dysphagia, 85% had complete 
resolution of dysphagia within 10 weeks after injury.151 Therefore 
15% of patients may have prolonged dysphagia after discharge 
from the hospital setting or IPR. Continued dysphagia treatment 
in the outpatient setting, including additional objective evalua -
tions (MBS or FEES), is recommended.
Cognitive Concerns
All burn survivors should be given a cognitive screen and, if it is 
positive, receive continued cognitive therapy by an SLP . Up to 
79% of burn survivors have cognitive communication deficits  
on admission to IPR. 75,138 Burn patients have lower memory 
scores on measures of cognitive function compared to other 
populations in rehabilitation.138 This holds true after accounting 
for confounders. The most common etiology is delirium, but 
other etiologies may include decreased oxygenation to the 
hippocampi from smoke or chemical inhalation, stress of the 
injury and hospital setting, and medications. 138 For those with 
deficits, speech-language therapy is initiated and typically dosed 5 
to 7 days per week in 30- to 60-minute sessions. The treatment 
focuses on maintaining sustained attention, memory strategies, 
functional problem solving, and carryover of techniques and 
treatments. Review of medications and their purpose upon 
discharge to home is also addressed to ensure adherence. The 
frequency of SLP sessions can be decreased as cognitive status 
improves. However, in 27% of survivors, deficits can persist past 
discharge (similar to data described in post-ICU survivors). 75 
Outpatient referral for cognitive SLP service is recommended for 
those still with deficits at the time of discharge.
For burn survivors discharged to IPR, a higher cognitive score 
on admission is associated with a higher functional score at  
discharge. Patients with higher cognitive scores on admission to 
IPR are also less likely to be discharged back to acute care within 
the first 72 hours of their IPR stay.11
Postburn Pruritus
Pruritis often accompanies neuropathic pain in burn survivors. 88 
Pruritis affects between 70% and 90% of adult burn survivors and 
reduces quality of life. 26,157 Intrinsic factors that may confer 
greater itch severity postburn include female sex, older age, a 
larger TBSA, and those with burns located on the head/face/
neck.134 Although no consensus has been reached, pathophysiol -
ogy of postburn pruritis is postulated to include pathways involved 
in regeneration of tissue 157 as well as a separate and specialized 
pathway to transmit neuropathic itch.88 Pruritis sensation is expe-
rienced due to the activation of cutaneous itch receptors by sub -
stances present in the burn scar, such as histamine, opioids, tumor 
necrosis factor-alpha, cytokines, proteases, and acetylcholine. 
These neuropeptides are needed to promote epidermal, connec -
tive tissue, and vascular cell proliferation; however, they also in -
crease sensitivity of the pruriceptors. 34,157 Sensation from the 
pruriceptors is transmitted by afferent C-fibers. 127 The signal is 
relayed to the dorsal root ganglia and then the dorsal horn of the 
spinal cord, crossing to the contralateral side and further con -
ducted centrally by the spinothalamic tract.127
Gabapentin has been shown to reduce pruritis scores in burn 
survivors,64 as has a separate study on pregabalin, 5 suggesting a

to neuropathic pain postburn. This is further supported by evi -

5,34 A topical com-

and 5% lidocaine has also shown improvement in postburn neu -
ropathic itch.
96 T opical emollients and massage also have a role in 
treatment. Adjunct treatments with botulinum toxin, naltrexone, 
tricyclic antidepressants, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, 
and behavioral therapies have also been described but are not 
widely used.
34,157
Neuropathy
Between 10% and 37% of burn survivors are affected by periph -
eral neuropathies.34,142 Diagnosis of focal and generalized periph-
eral neuropathy is made with careful neurologic examination and/
or electrodiagnostic testing.
55 As in the general population, neu-
ropathies may be managed in IPR setting with PT , OT , splinting, 
and neuropathic pain medication.
174 Median mononeuropathy is 
the most common mononeuropathy in this population.174 Causes 
of mononeuropathy include direct thermal or electrical injury, 
damage related to escharotomy or fasciotomy, soft tissue edema, 
metabolic derangements, immobilization, and heterotopic ossifi -
cation (HO).
174 Additional risk factors can include preexisting 
injuries, older age, history of alcoholism, and diabetes melli -
tus.157,174 Generalized peripheral neuropathy, similar to critical 
illness neuropathy, can affect 3% to 30% of patients due in part 
to circulating inflammatory mediators from the body’s systemic 
response to burn injury leading to nerve injury. This manifests 
clinically as paresthesia and distal weakness.
157 T o minimize iatro-
genic contribution to neuropathy, care should be taken during 
surgical and postoperative positioning to reduce traction on the 
brachial plexus resulting in plexopathy. Pneumatic inflation to 
minimize surgical blood loss should be carefully positioned and 
timed to reduce the risk of compression neuropraxia. Restraints 
should be padded and discontinued as soon as possible.
Heterotopic Ossification
HO is defined as mature bone deposited in areas of soft tissue, 
including muscle, ligaments, and tendon.
142 HO affects between 
3.5% and 5.6% of persons with burns, 98,117 with the posterior 
elbow being the most common site (Fig. 27.5).98 It is possible that 
smaller areas of HO may not cause pain or functional limitations 
and are therefore not diagnosed. The diagnosis of HO can be 
suspected clinically by pain and restricted ROM around the  
affected joint, a palpable firm lump, and swelling or erythema. It 
should be noted that HO will likely be evident clinically before 
corresponding changes on x-ray or triple phase bone scan. Ultra -
sound may also be helpful for diagnosis.
100 In one study, the me-
dian time to clinical development of HO in burn survivors was  
37 days (range 30 to 40 days), and the median time to radiologic 
development was 49 days (range 38 to 118 days).
117 Risk factors 
for development of HO include greater than 30% TBSA burn 
and arm burns requiring grafting.
105 The likelihood of HO also 
increases with each return trip to the operating room and each day 
requiring mechanical ventilation.
105
Articles since the 1970s have documented progressive ROM 
exercises to reduce the occurrence and prevent the progression of 
HO in patients with a variety of diagnoses (joint replacement, 
spinal cord injury, brain injury, etc.).
167 However, a small study 
specific to burn injury by Crawford et al. showed active ROM 
within the pain-free arc was helpful in reducing the progression of 
HO. Those who were engaged only in passive ROM showed pro-
gression to bone maturity and need for surgical removal.
38 In 
retrospect, this may be confounded by the severity of injury, abil-
ity of patient participation due to sedation, and length of required 
ventilation. Therefore in IPR, active progressive ROM guided by 
therapists is recommended with gentle prolonged stretch near the 
end range. While there is no consensus on treatment, NSAIDs 
have been shown to help reduce the progression of HO in burn 
patients but are not helpful prophylactically.
105 Bisphosphonates 
and focused radiation treatment have not shown consistent ben -
efit and can have long-term safety concerns. 105 Surgical resection

Amputation
The incidence of reported amputation as a result of burn injury is 
between 2%
71 and 6%. 165 While fire/flame remains the most 
likely etiology of burns requiring amputations, electrical injury 
has a higher incidence of amputation.
165 Other contributing fac-
tors to amputation include male sex, age range 18 to 64 years, 
higher TBSA, presence of infection, and full-thickness injury.
13,165 
The majority ( 65%) of postburn amputations are considered 
minor (to include fingers or toes), and the remaining 35% are 
considered major (to include transradial, transhumeral, transtib -
ial, or transfemoral levels).
13
Amputation is classified as early when it is within 2 to 3 days of 
admission and is usually undertaken due to obvious tissue necrosis, 
nonsalvageable trauma such as vascular disruption or fracture, or 
infection. Early amputation is associated with decreased infection, 
mortality rates, and hospital length of stay for burn patients.
190 
Amputation may be delayed/late (postburn day 3 and on) to  
determine amount of salvageable tissue remaining or feasibility of 
reconstruction.
190 If adequate healthy tissue is available, a free-flap 
tissue transfer may reduce the risk of future amputation.89
Timing of prosthetic training and rehabilitation in the burn 
survivor is similar to amputees without burn injury. It includes 
complete closure of all incisions and initiation of edema manage -
ment and shaping systems, such as compression socks or dressings 
or rigid removable orthoses.
54 Prosthetic fit after burn-related 
amputation presents unique challenges. The presence of amputa -
tion is a major contributor to contracture formation in burn 
survivors.
156 Other considerations for postburn amputees include 
the likeliness of adherent skin tissue due to reduced elasticity of 
grafted skin, reduced sensation or hypersensitivity, and reduced 
ability to perform desensitization due to grafted skin or ROM 
limitations.
54 Burn-related heterotopic ossification can also be a 
confounding factor in prosthetic fit.63 The prosthetic prescription 
should account for these parameters, as well as viability of skin 
grafts or flaps (including vascular anastomoses), potential repeated 
skin breakdown, and concomitant joint contractures due to scar 
or positioning.
89,135 All amputees should engage in a progressive 
wear schedule to ensure tolerance over burned skin and grafts to 
minimize shear and risk of skin breakdown.
54
Socket design should ensure pressure is in load-tolerant areas. 
T otal surface bearing sockets are preferred to patella tendon bear-
ing sockets in burn-related transtibial amputees to increase the 
load bearing surface.
54 If required for proper fit, the liner interface 
can be custom molded to accommodate skin grafts or flaps. Offset 
plates can be used to increase stability or reduce the influence of 
contractures on gait mechanics. Suspension systems should con -
sider skin integrity and potential for shear damage. For example, 
body-powered upper extremity suspension harnesses may cause 
significant tissue damage over areas of graft, and a myoelectric 
fitting (although not typically first line) may be required. In gen -
eral, suction suspension is preferred to reduce the influence of 
shear on compromised tissue.
54 The remainder of the prosthetic 
components should be determined by the survivor’s functional 
status, based on Medicare Functional Classification K.
29
Hypertrophic Scarring
Cutaneous scarring after burn injury is ubiquitous and in up to 
70% of survivors, resulting in visually and functionally undesirable 
hypertrophic scarring.
20 Hypertrophic scarring is a pathologic 
closure of burn injury, with architecture and composition distinct 
from normal skin due to cellular and molecular abnormalities. 
Hypertrophic scarring ( Fig. 27.6) is characterized by elevation 
compared to surrounding (uninjured) skin, coloration changes that 
can be hyper- or hypopigmented and/or erythematous, stiff with 
reduced elasticity, contracted, painful, and pruritic.
15 In addition, 
hypertrophic scarring, especially elevated hyperpigmented scar, has 
been associated with increased incidences of pain.
162 Many conse-
quences of hypertrophic scarring including skin fragility, dryness, 
open wounds, pain, itch, skin sensitivity or decreased sensation, 
and loss of function (contractures, tightness, decreased ROM), but 
can improve some persisted 2 years after injury.
65
Factors associated with increased likelihood of hypertrophic 
scaring include younger age; burns due to fire, flame, or flash; and 
markers of severity of burn injury, including percentage of TBSA

burned and grafted, presence of inhalation injury and length of 
mechanical ventilation, hospital length of stay, number of surgical 
interventions needed, and need for IPR.
56,65 Burn wounds that 
take longer than 2 to 3 weeks to close are also predisposed to 
hypertrophic scarring.
136 Hypertrophic scarring is a result of im -
balanced pathways required for normal healing. The process of 
wound healing includes hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation 
(including angiogenesis and extracellular matrix deposition), and 
remodeling.
109 Multiple cascades and cell signaling pathways are 
involved but altered. Fibroblasts deposit collagen in the prolifera-
tion phase; however, overexpression of type 1 and decreased expres-
sion of type 3 are noted in burn wounds.173 Decreased appropriate 
degradation of collagen is also noted during remodeling.173 Fibro-
blasts differentiate into myofibroblasts, which are responsible  
for wound contraction and remodeling. Myofibroblasts then also 
contribute multiple inflammatory cytokines to the healing milieu, 
perpetuating the cycle. In hypertrophic scarring, both fibroblasts 
and myofibroblasts are resistant to typical apoptosis during  
remodeling that follows appropriate wound closure.
32
T reatment options for hypertrophic burn scars may reduce but 
not eliminate the negative effects on cosmesis and function. Burn 
compression garments at 20 to 30 mm Hg compression provided 
within 60 days of injury, and worn consistently for 6 months, can 
reduce pigmentation, thickness, pain, and itch.
99 Pressure gar-
ments are thought to exert their effect by restricting blood, oxy -
gen, and cytokine migration to the scar. 161 The relative hypoxia 
leads to cellular death of fibroblasts, reduction in extracellular 
matrix production, and increased expression of the collagenase 
stimulator, prostaglandin E2, therefore degrading excess collagen.
161 
There is a new debate on the effectiveness of pressure garments  
in effecting long-term outcome. One must note that garments do 
not work over body parts that move or are soft. They are also quite 
expensive, and compliance rates may vary. Silicone sheeting 
within pressure garments may be helpful to reduce insensate water 
loss as dehydration.
107,111
T opical steroids contribute to vasoconstriction and reduction 
of the ongoing inflammatory cascade that contributes to hyper -
trophic scarring. Intralesional injection of triamcinolone histori -
cally is a mainstay of treatment, although it has variable effect and 
a component of undesirable effects of skin atrophy and telangiec-
tasia. More recent studies, including an injectable combination of 
triamcinolone and 5-fluorouracil, showed improved scar height, 
pliability, and pigmentation.
192
Botulinum injections into hypertrophic scarring caused by burn 
injury have been promising in small studies, 170 with reduction in 
vascularity and height from unaffected skin and increased pliability. 
There was also benefit in symptoms of itch and pain at the  
scar. Multiple meta-analyses on safety and efficacy of botulinum 
toxin have also been favorable in reducing the appearance and char-
acteristics of hypertrophic scarring.
139,181 There is also described 
augmented benefit of combining botulinum toxin with steroid in-
jection.
16,101 Histopathologically, botulinum toxin is postulated to 
have its effect by reducing muscular tension on the wound bed, 
reducing fibroblast differentiation into myoblasts, and influencing 
downstream pathways involved in collagen production.
10,90
T reatment of healed burn scars with fractionated carbon diox-
ide (fCO2) lasers is safe and effective in reducing visible charac -
teristics of scar and treating associated symptoms of pain, itch, 
and perceived tightness.
106 Subsequently, measures of burn-related 
quality of life increased.82 fCO2 lasers ablate water within abnor-
mal collagen in scar tissue, forming microscopic holes perpen -
dicular to the scar, which encourages remodeling. 76 fCO2 laser 
treatments are typically done on an outpatient basis. 36,82 These 
treatments also decrease the number of reconstructive procedures 
required and are quickly becoming the treatment of choice for 
burn-related hypertrophic scarring.
36,82 If the desired result with 
multimodal scar treatment is not achieved, surgical intervention 
with incisional scar-release techniques or tissue or muscle grafts 
can be employed.
166
Contracture and Prevention
Burn scar contracture is defined as a condition in which skin is 
replaced with pathologic scar tissue of insufficient extensibility and 
length resulting in loss of motion or tissue alignment of an associ-
ated joint or anatomic structure.
123 Contractures can alter ROM, 
reduce wound healing, and impair functional status, including 
decrement in mobility and ADL, particularly those requiring fine 
motor movements.
66 In order of declining prevalence, contracture 
most frequently develops over shoulders, elbows, wrists, ankles, 
and knees.
66 Maintaining a functional position while patients are 
in bed or immobilized both in acute care and IPR is frequently 
cited (Fig. 27.7). In the lower extremities, this includes keeping 
the hips at neutral, knees in extension, and ankles at 90 degrees  
of dorsiflexion. The trunk should be straight without lateral bend-
ing or flexion. Upper limb positioning should keep the shoulders 
abducted, elbows in approximately 30 degrees of flexion, wrists  
in 20 to 40 degrees of extension, with metacarpophalangeal joints 
in 40 to 60 degrees of flexion. The thumb should be in radial 
abduction. Proximal and distal interphalangeal joints should be 
fully extended.
30
Stretching is utilized to combat unopposed wound contraction 
and tissue shortening as burned and grafted skin heals. Gentle, 
slow, prolonged stretching is preferred over high-velocity stretch-
ing to reduce injury to fragile tissue.
30 Stretching should begin 
during the postoperative phase to ensure adequate tissue length to 
allow for full joint ROM. It should also progress to maximum toler-
ated for improved outcome. For example, shoulder positioning in

more severe injuries, as measured by longer length of stay, more 
hours of rehabilitation per day, more edema therapy, larger TBSA, 
surgical release, inhalation injury, ventilator days, and days of  
sedation or bedrest. Wrist, elbow, and ankle are the joints most 
likely to be splinted, and knees were least likely. 91 However, 
splinting is not without controversy in the literature, given the 
fact that tension on a graft can induce fibroblast production of 
collagen and differentiation into myofibroblasts, 158 promoting 
hypertrophic scar. There is also not a consistent recommendation 
for type of splint or wear time, when to initiate splinting, and the 
degree of splinting.
The only practice guideline regarding splinting in the existing 
literature is that orthotic use should be considered as a treatment 
choice for improving ROM or reducing contracture in adults who 
have sustained a burn injury.123 Despite advancements in multiple 
aspects of burn and rehabilitation care, some patients are discharged 
with ROM loss. The incidence of joint contractures remains  
between 38% and 54%, 66,116 which is similar to nonburned  
patients with prolonged ICU stays. 35 Up to one-third of patients 
with a major burn leave the hospital with a contracture. 67 Skin 
grafting over a joint, higher TBSA necessitating grafting, male sex, 
concomitant medical complications, and neuropathy were more 
likely to develop contracture. 66,156 However, as the time from 
burn injury increases, the incidence of scar contracture decreases 
(due to therapy, surgical correction, maturation, etc.).116,158
Consideration of Inpatient Rehabilitation
Annually, 9000 burn survivors are admitted to IPR hospitals to 
improve their functional outcomes after burn injury. 169 Burn  
injuries are compliant with the 60% rule, designated as one of the 
13 diagnoses requiring intensive IPR per Centers for Medicare 
and Medicaid Services.27 Burn survivors in IPR demonstrate out-
comes similar to those admitted due to other etiologies, including 
stroke, acquired brain injury, spinal cord dysfunction, orthopedic 
conditions, and other neurologic conditions. 169 The number of 
burn survivors discharged to IPR can vary from 8% to 26% of 
patients admitted to the hospital for burn injury, depending on the 
state where they are located and the percentage of body surface 
involved.69 It is noted that IPR for these patients helps to reduce 
acute center length of stay and improves functional outcomes. 169 
IPR is shown to confer a benefit to older survivors (.60 years old) 
compared to discharge to a skilled nursing facility (SNF) 120 and 
practice models should include IPR as a discharge option for 
older adults, considering their longer length of stay and medical 
comorbidities.129 Discharge to IPR instead of SNF allows for  
additional time for burn healing with onsite wound care, inten -
sive therapies to improve functional status, and thus potentially 
less burden of care for caregivers.120 Depending on the functional 
limitations of the individual patient, a typical IPR length of stay 
is 2 to 4 weeks, followed by discharge to home.
Burn survivors should meet standard InterQual criteria to be 
considered for IPR, including ability to tolerate and participate in 
greater than 3 hours of therapy daily, ongoing medical issues to be 
managed by a physiatrist with specialized rehabilitation training, 
and care needs that cannot be coordinated in a subacute nursing 
facility environment.28 Characteristics of survivors requiring com-
prehensive IPR services include those with more complex injuries 
(including large burns [ .20% TBSA], especially involving the 
hands and feet), ROM restriction, inhalation injury, dysphagia, 
and prolonged acute course (including mechanical ventilation).169 
Higher age, hospitalization at a burn center as opposed to an 
outlying hospital, and possession of health insurance were also 
identified as factors increasing the likelihood of IPR stay after 
acute hospitalization for burn injury.69
All acute surgical interventions should be completed prior to 
admission to IPR to minimize interruption of the comprehensive 
therapy program. Burn survivors that transferred back to acute 
care and were subsequently readmitted to IPR had a lower func -
tional level on admission and discharge. They were also more 
likely to return to acute care at final discharge, instead of to the 
community.46
Therapy and Rehabilitation
IPR therapy is provided by PTs and OTs who preferably have 
been certified by the ABA. Burn survivors may have experienced 
comorbid conditions requiring immobility, such as prolonged 
ventilation due to inhalation or other respiratory conditions,  
aggressive sedation for pain management, additional traumatic 
injuries, or mobility and ROM restrictions after recent grafting. 
Physiatrists and therapists should be aware of any ROM and 
weight-bearing restrictions.
Early therapy focuses on elongation, stretching, maintaining 
ROM, contracture prevention, edema reduction, evaluation for 
bracing, and more dynamic activities such as transferring to a 
chair, maintaining sitting position, and supported standing. These 
activities, while not conferring a mortality benefit, are associated 
with reduced incidence of pneumonia, deep vein thrombosis,  
reduced number of hospital and ICU days, and improved periph-
eral and respiratory muscle strength.83 In addition, early mobility 
lays the foundation for movement and exercise in IPR.
As the survivor progresses, treatment focus changes to active 
exercise, mobility, and endurance training, as well as ADL and 
independent ADL, independence with ROM, skin care, and care-
giver training (Fig. 27.8).
Exercise plays a vital role in the rehabilitation of patients with 
severe burns. Survivors experience several years of reduced exercise 
capacity and reduced pulmonary function following burn injury.132 
T o counteract these changes, exercise programs should be indi -
vidualized for each survivor and their functional needs. The phys-
iatrist reviews the exercise plan and progress toward goals to ensure 
optimal outcome. Exercise training in those surviving severe 
burns demonstrated improvement in lean muscle mass, strength, 
reduced requirement for contracture release, and improvement in 
physical fitness and maximal oxygen update.45
Exercise programs should begin while hospitalized and con -
tinue after discharge. The ABA published guidelines for exercise 
prescription following burn injury, which include a resistance 
and/or aerobic exercise program under supervision for a mini -
mum of 6 to 12 weeks,112 progressing to independent programs.
Aerobic Training
The stress response to burns includes hypermetabolic catabo -
lism, circulating catecholamines, cortisol, and proinflammatory 
factors, and results in muscle wasting and cardiac dysfunc -
tion.118 Survivors typically have elevated heart rate and cardiac 
output, as well as higher risk of cardiovascular diagnoses such  
as hypertension, coronary artery disease, and heart failure up to

Resistance Training
The safety of adding resistance training in burn survivors within 
the acute care setting has been established.62 The addition of resis-
tance training or sham resistance training in two matched and 
compared groups found no differences in adverse outcomes or 
increased length of stay due to complications. There was improve-
ment in the strength of the upper extremities in those who  
received resistance training compared to the standard PT group.
62 
Since safety and efficacy have been established in the acute care 
setting, presumably when survivors are more ill, the safety and 
efficacy of exercise in IPR and as an outpatient is implied.
Resistance training programs are used to strengthen individual 
muscles using machines, bands, free weights, or body weights.
62,118 
Therapists should provide initial instruction on form, technique, 
and safety, considering each individual’s pain and current func -
tional level.
62 Typical resistance exercise programs in burn survi -
vors target large muscle groups crossing multiple joints (such as 
legs, chest, back) or isolate specific movements (such as biceps 
curls or triceps press). Strengthening of the core should also be 
incorporated. Gradual progression is recommended ( Table 27.2) 
with further increases after 12 weeks, by increasing the weight or 
repetitions by 5% to 10% each week.
118 This program can be 
initiated in the IPR setting, with instructions for continuation 
during outpatient PT or as a self-directed program.
Psychological Considerations of Survivorship
Screening and treatment of burn survivors’ psychological state is 
paramount. Burn survivors endorse preinjury mental health con -
cerns at rates higher than the general population,
53,126 particularly 
with depression and substance use disorder,102 which can be com-
pounded by postinjury fear, anxiety, pain, delirium, and grief. 175 
Additionally, even years after initial injury, survivors report  
decreased quality of life and poorer physical and mental health3,17 
compared to nonburned individuals, making screening and treat-
ment for psychological concerns essential parts of recovery.
Recommendations from the ABA Psychological Outcomes 
Consensus Committee in 2013 advocate screening for psycho -
logical outcomes as a quality metric to focus on data collection 
and elevation of psychosocial care for burn survivors.
60 In addi-
tion, recommendations include screening for depression and 
acute stress disorder (ASD) symptoms within 48 hours of the 
survivors’ ability to participate in the interview, and at least once 
prior to discharge from the hospital. When returning for outpa -
tient care, all survivors should have repeat screening (for depres -
sion, ASD, and posttraumatic stress disorder [PTSD]) at their 
first clinic visit and subsequent visits as needed. All ABA-certified 
burn centers are required to outline their process for referral when 
survivors screen positive.
60
Delirium
Acutely, ICU delirium is characterized by change in baseline cog-
nition and psychomotor status, with fluctuations in intensity and 
severity throughout the day, not attributable to a separate medical 
condition such as neurologic, infectious, withdrawal symptoms, 
hypoxia, and the like.
8 Delirium is common among burn survi -
vors, with an incidence of 47% of those admitted to the ICU. 1 
This increases to 77% in those who also require ventilation in the 
ICU setting.
4 Interventions to reduce harm from delirium (in -
cluding mortality and length of ICU stay) 12 center on restorative 
day/night scheduling, increased daytime mobility, reorientation, 
presence of familiar family members and friends, optimization of 
pain management, and minimizing deliriogenic medications.
12 
Judicious use of medications to treat symptoms of delirium, such 
as paranoia, agitation, risk of dislodging medical devices, or treat-
ments, and harm to the survivor and staff can be considered.
143
Anxiety
Anxiety and pain are often an interlinked and perpetuating cycle, 
whereby anxiety in anticipation of pain affects the perception of 
pain,
85,168 and the presence of pain increases anxiety. Interven -
tions can include medicating prior to procedural interventions 
and frequent provision of information about expected schedules, 
daily activities, procedures, and outcomes.
168 Anxiety in burn 
survivors may also be a direct manifestation of the trauma itself, 
as well as concerns about financial stability, and survivors ques-
tioning their ability to reengage in family, work, and social roles. 
Anxiety tends to be highest in the acute phase but remains present 
in at least 20% of survivors at 6 months. Long-term symptoms 
directly related to the injury can persist for up to 2 years.
121
Depression
Postburn injury depression is difficult to quantify in the literature 
due to inconsistent measurement methods and reporting.
177,178 
However, depression postburn injury can be present acutely, 
triggered by realization of consequences of injury, including 
functional limitations and altered sense of self. It can be closely 
correlated with survivors’ preinjury incidence of depression.
102 
Rates of in-hospital depression range from 53% to 15% and tend 
to be more severe acutely and decrease as time from injury 
elapses.
137 At 1 year postinjury, 13% to 23% of survivors report 
symptoms consistent with major depression.178 A greater propor-
tion endorse psychological distress without strictly meeting the 
diagnostic criteria for depression. Importantly, depression after 
burn injury has been found to correlate with decreased physical 
functioning 1 year after injury.
176 T reatment cornerstones include 
CBT and medications.
Acute Stress Disorder and  
Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
ASD presents as hyperarousal and reexperiencing negative events 
with concomitant negative changes in cognition and mood within 
3 to 10 days of onset of exposure or experience of a traumatic 
event.
8 Prevalence in burn survivors ranges from 10% to 23% 76 
and is predicted by female sex, presence of visible injury, and

preinjury psychological status. ASD within the hospital setting is 
predictive of PTSD symptoms up to 2 years postinjury. 59 Symp-
toms of PTSD include thought intrusions (distressing memories 
or dreams, flashbacks), avoidance of memories, stimuli or remind-
ers of the trauma, and negative changes in mood such as irritabil-
ity, increased startle response, difficulty with sleep, and feelings of 
blame and isolation. 8 These symptoms must present after expo -
sure or experience of a trauma, last more than 1 month, and lead 
to distress or functional deficits. PTSD has a higher incidence in 
burn survivors than in the trauma population in general, with 
rates recorded as 15% to 45% 1 year following trauma.59,178 There 
is an association between higher levels of posttraumatic stress and 
chronic pain and itch. 178 T reatment includes trauma-informed 
psychological therapy (CBT , group therapy), eye movement  
desensitization and reprocessing, and medications.
Sleep Disturbance
Due to physical discomfort such as pain, itch, and emotional 
distress, it is not surprising that many survivors have disturbance 
in sleep. The prevalence is 40% to 73% and may persist for 
months to years following burn injury. 97 Sleep hygiene practice 
information and medication interventions can be helpful. 153
Body Image and Appearance Satisfaction
Coping with changes in appearance, body image, and the inevi -
table effect these have on self-esteem and sexuality remain a great 
challenge for burn survivors, 184 especially females and those who 
place great value on personal appearance and attractiveness. 95  
Female sex, higher TBSA burn, and importance of appearance 
predict body dissatisfaction of survivors, which in turn affects 
psychosocial function at 1 year. 172 Circularly, psychosocial com -
ponents, including depression, self-acceptance, and comfort with 
social integration, are also important factors in body satisfaction 
after burn.95 Therefore routine psychological screening, including 
body image and distress, should be utilized both in the hospital 
and at follow-up visits and appropriate referrals made. 172
Sexuality and Sexual Functioning
Sexual functioning is intricately tied to body appearance and sat -
isfaction and should be inquired about by the comprehensive 
burn team early in care utilizing open-ended questions. 122,175 
Unfortunately, only a small proportion (14%) of practitioners feel 
comfortable and qualified to initiate this discussion. 145 However, 
all practitioners working with burn survivors and their partners 
should be aware that concerns about sexuality and sexual func -
tioning are common and important to them and can have long-
term impact on quality of life. 37 The changes brought about by 
burn injury affect sexual function in survivors. For example, scars 
leading to decreased sensitivity or hypersensitivity of skin, pain, 
changes to ROM, or damage to genitalia can make sexual func -
tion challenging.39 Fear or rejection or body image concerns can 
also affect sexual confidence. Side effects from medications, such 
as beta blockers or selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, can 
decrease libido.39 Circulating catecholamines and hypermetabolic 
hormones may interfere with sex hormone release.122 Practitioners 
working with burn survivors should be aware of these effects on 
sexual functioning and be willing to initiate the conversation and 
provide therapeutic guidance, whether from OTs to optimize com-
fort and safety with positioning, physicians making medication 
adjustments, or psychologists to address body acceptance  
and communication.175 Referrals to online resources such as the 
Phoenix Society for Burn Survivors or in-person or virtual special-
ized sex therapists or psychiatrists should also be within the realm 
of burn practitioners.131
Social and Community Reintegration
Burn survivors cite social anxiety and strain of interacting with 
those who do not understand the long-term sequelae of burn, while 
they themselves are coping with physical and psychological changes, 
to be the most difficult part of community reintegration.19 Reinte-
gration is particularly challenging for those with facial involve -
ment146 and those with poor physical function, who even 5 years 
after injury report feelings of depression, social isolation, and loss of 
peer group.119 Survivors also have difficulty meeting new friends, 
making friends, and forging intimate relationships.149
Programs to assist with acceptance of appearance, self-esteem, 
and social skills following burn injury are also excellent resources 
to assist with community integration after burn. The Phoenix 
Society for Burn Survivors has developed multiple programs to 
facilitate social and community reintegration, including Survivors 
Offering Assistance with Recovery (SOAR), where screened and 
trained survivors provide peer mentorship, knowledge, empathy, 
and support to those more recently injured. SOAR mentors pro -
vide support throughout recovery in one-on-one visits, group 
meetings online or in person, live chats, and social media post -
ings.131 Other programs developed by the Phoenix Society in -
clude Beyond Surviving: T ools for Thriving After Burn Injury, 
which promotes life strategies to be comfortable, confident, and 
competent in any social, work, or school situation. Changing 
Faces is another program designed to facilitate social skills, self-
esteem, and community integration using the 3-2-1-GO model, 
which encourages survivors to practice how they will respond to 
others’ reactions to their appearance.124 In the 3-2-1-Go model, 
survivors compose three things they will do if someone stares at 
them, two answers if someone asks what happened, and one thing 
to think if someone turns away. These programs help survivors 
cope with self-consciousness that may occur with public reactions 
to their changed appearance and promote self-efficacy.
Return to Work
For survivors of employment age, return to work is one signifier 
of recovery. Analysis of the Burn Model Systems National Data -
base demonstrated that those employed preburn are most likely to 
return to work in the 12 months postburn. 25,189 Survivors who 
participated in IPR programs had a higher likelihood of returning 
to employment 1 year postinjury compared to those who received 
rehabilitation at a SNF , long-term care hospital, or other extended 
care facility, despite a more severe injury. 50 White, non-Hispanic 
survivors were more likely to be employed postburn than other 
ethnic groups. 25,189 Multiple published reviews have identified 
factors associated with unemployment postburn (in those who 
were employed at the time of injury), including older age, female 
sex, longer inpatient hospitalization, elevated levels of pain, and 
government (vs. private) insurance. 25,103,140 Those who were in -
jured at work were twice as likely to be unemployed at 1 year as 
those burned outside of work. 154 Other studies have determined 
psychological factors, such as premorbid anxiety, depression, and 
alcoholism, and amputation status are also important predictors 
of return to work. 26,52 The type of employment and the physical

demands thereof affected the time to return to work.21 Those with 
fewer physical requirements for their job were able to return more 
quickly. Additionally, survivors who developed contractures at the 
time of hospital discharge were also less likely to be employed 
postburn than those who did not have contracture formation. 130 
There is conflicting conclusion in the literature as to whether  
return to work status was influenced by the size of the burn based 
on TBSA, the involvement (and to what degree) of hands, 21,140 
and whether workers’ compensation was utilized. 25,189 These in-
consistencies suggest that the psychological factors associated with 
return to work play a much larger role than physical factors. The 
most common barriers to returning to work include pain, neuro -
logic symptoms, and impaired mobility, which are most typically 
seen in more severe injury. 154 Only 37% of those who were able 
to return to work could engage in the same job without accom -
modations,23 further underscoring the importance of physiatrists 
in the return-to-work process.
Summary
Burn rehabilitation is a lifelong process for survivors. Physiatrists 
are uniquely positioned to work as part of an interdisciplinary 
team, identifying and managing complications of burn injury 
across the care spectrum, including inpatient, IPR, and outpatient 
stages of recovery, to reduce the systemic impact of the injury and 
improve quality of life. Burn physiatrists implement interventions 
for pain, itch, neuropathy, delirium, psychosocial coping and 
mood, and nutritional needs. Burn physiatrists also oversee and 
direct a rehabilitation program focusing on scar management, 
contracture prevention, dysphagia treatment, evaluation and 
treatment of cognitive impairments, and focus on aerobic and 
resistive exercises to optimize functional recovery. Burn physiat -
rists can also be instrumental in providing resources for coping 
with changing abilities and perceptions of self, while focusing on 
community reintegration and return to work.
